text.title: pytest Mocking — Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every python-pytest-mocking application must obey.

rule.statement: Use the mocker fixture from pytest-mock. Do not call unittest.mock.patch directly in tests.
rule.rationale: mocker auto-cleans patches after every test; manual patch.start/stop or with-blocks are boilerplate and error-prone.

rule.statement: Patch where the function is used, not where it is defined: mocker.patch('apps.orders.services.send_mail') when orders.services does 'from emails.utils import send_mail'.
rule.rationale: Python binds names at import; patching the definition has no effect on the consumer.

rule.statement: Use autospec=True (or create_autospec) so mocks match the real signature. Reject calls with wrong args at test time.
rule.rationale: Without autospec, a mock accepts any args — silent signature drift between code and test.

rule.statement: Mock async callables with AsyncMock. Never with MagicMock.
rule.rationale: MagicMock returns a sync value when awaited (warning: coroutine was never awaited); AsyncMock returns a coroutine.

rule.statement: Use mocker.spy(obj, 'method') when you want the real implementation to run but also record calls.
rule.rationale: Spy gives observability without changing behaviour — ideal for verifying that production code actually called the dependency.

rule.statement: Do not patch Model.objects.* in service tests. Use a real DB fixture with savepoint rollback.
rule.rationale: Mocking the ORM tests the mock, not the code; passes even when querysets are broken.
